7. The hardened configuration is captured in `docs/RELEASING.md` under a new "Release Environment" section (extending the file created in Story 1.1) so future maintainers can audit or restore state, including the exact `gh api` commands to inspect and re-apply it.
```

content.text:
I've verified the key facts. Here are my findings.

## Findings

- **The diff text does not match the "Active rules (5)" table — only 5 rules listed, but the text claims the ruleset has 5 rules yet the commit history ("create release GitHub environment with required-reviewer gate" and earlier "document hardened main ruleset") and user memory around this work suggest the count may drift.** (drift, suspected) — line 16: "Active rules (5):" is hard-coded. If any later PR adds a sixth rule (e.g. `required_signatures`, `required_linear_history`, `required_deployments`) the table will silently drift from actual state. No automated check. Verified against `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/docs/RELEASING.md:16`.

- **"Required status checks (7)" will drift the moment `quality.yaml` gains or renames a job.** (drift, verified) — lines 26–34 hard-code job names, but `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/.github/workflows/quality.yaml` is the source of truth (I verified jobs: `prettier`, `eslint`, `markdownlint`, `validate` matrix `[ubuntu-latest, windows-latest]`, `python` matrix `[ubuntu-latest, windows-latest]` — these match). The doc tells a maintainer to "update both in the same PR," but there is no pre-commit or CI check that fails when the two lists disagree. A rename in `quality.yaml` without a RELEASING.md edit leaves the doc wrong and (per the doc itself) can let merges silently pass without the renamed check.

- **README's "Release Process" section header matches the docs file title ("Releasing SKF"), not the README h2 convention.** (drift, suspected) — `README.md:178` says "## Release Process" but `docs/RELEASING.md:2` says `title: Releasing SKF`. Minor, but a reader Ctrl-F'ing "Releasing" in the README won't find it, and future edits may desynchronize further.

- **Heredoc delimiter `<<'JSON'` with `--input -` is robust, but re-running the "(1) Create or update the environment" block on an already-configured environment silently drops the `main` policy if a later user naively omits step 2.** (idempotency, verified against diff lines 103–121) — The environment PUT preserves `custom_branch_policies: true` but does NOT re-populate the `/deployment-branch-policies` list. That list is separate state. If someone runs step 1 a second time (thinking "this is idempotent, let me re-apply") without step 2, the environment still has `main` in its allow-list because the separate resource was not touched — OK. But if they run step 1 after someone else has DELETEd all branch policies, step 1 does not restore them. The doc acknowledges the two-call pattern but does not state that step 2 is also non-idempotent: POSTing `{"name": "main"}` a second time returns 422 (branch policy already exists). A fresh maintainer re-running the block will get a confusing 422 and may think the environment is broken.

- **Step 2 POST is not idempotent — re-running after partial success yields HTTP 422.** (idempotency, verified against lines 117–120) — GitHub's `POST /environments/{env}/deployment-branch-policies` with a duplicate name returns `422 Unprocessable Entity`. The doc presents the two steps as a "Restore / re-apply" block with no guidance on what to do when step 1 succeeds and step 2 fails mid-sequence, or when the block is run twice. A maintainer copy-pasting during an incident will see 422 and not know whether they are safe.

- **`<POLICY_ID>` placeholder is a literal string in the shell.** (encoding/ordering, verified against lines 127–140) — The "Temporarily allowing a feature branch" block tells the reader to "capture the returned policy id" but the revoke snippet embeds `<POLICY_ID>` as a bash-unsafe shell token (`<` and `>` are redirection operators in bash). If a reader copy-pastes the revoke command without substituting, bash will attempt I/O redirection and fail in a confusing way (`bash: POLICY_ID: No such file or directory`). A `$POLICY_ID` convention plus an export in step 1 would be safer.

- **The "capture the returned policy id" step has no scripted capture.** (ordering, verified lines 128–134) — The POST output is shown as a comment (`# → { "id": <POLICY_ID>, ... }`) but the user is expected to eyeball the id and paste it later. If the POST output scrolls past, or if the user runs the POST in a subshell, they lose the id and cannot revoke. The allow-list then carries the feature branch indefinitely — which the doc itself calls "an unguarded hole." A `POLICY_ID=$(gh api ... --jq '.id')` pattern would make the revoke mechanically safe.

- **Revoke step is described as "the last step of the validation, not a follow-up" but the shell snippet has no trap/cleanup on failure between the two.** (ordering, verified lines 136–140) — If the validation cut between step 1 (add branch) and the revoke (delete branch) errors out, crashes the shell, or the maintainer Ctrl-C's, the feature branch remains on the allow-list. A `trap 'gh api --method DELETE ...' EXIT` pattern would close this gap; the doc's prose warning alone does not.

- **`gh api` calls assume authentication + scopes the doc never states.** (precondition, verified entire file) — Ruleset writes require `admin:repo` on the authenticating token; environment writes require `repo` scope + admin role. `gh auth status` is never mentioned. A maintainer with a read-only token will see opaque 403s from every command in the doc.

- **`jq` dependency in the "Restore from a saved baseline" block is not stated as a precondition.** (precondition, verified line 56) — `jq '{rules, bypass_actors}' baseline.json` assumes `jq` is installed. On a fresh macOS or Windows maintainer machine this may not be true. `gh api --jq` would keep the dep inside `gh`.

GitHub's PUT replaces the entire resource — if the server requires those fields (it does, per the API ref), this call will 422. If it doesn't, the ruleset may silently revert `enforcement` to the default or lose its target. The doc's own later paragraph says "the `rules` array is replaced wholesale — it is not merged server-side" but the restore snippet only restores rules, not the surrounding configuration.

- **Baseline.json source is unspecified.** (precondition, verified line 52–56) — "A JSON baseline captured before any change" — no guidance on when/how to capture it, or where it lives. If a maintainer needs this during an incident and no baseline exists, the whole section is dead. The repo has no checked-in baseline and no scripted capture, so the restore path is aspirational.

- **Ruleset id `13855503` and environment id `14347249917` are hard-coded — if either is deleted and recreated, the id changes and every `gh api .../rulesets/13855503` in the doc breaks.** (drift, verified lines 14, 47, 59–60, 69) — The doc has no fallback instruction like "if the id has changed, find it with `gh api repos/.../rulesets --jq '.[] | select(.name==\"Default\") | .id'`." A future recreate will leave the maintainer with a 404 and no remediation path.

- **`release` environment id `14347249917` is hard-coded, but the environment API is keyed by *name*, not id.** (drift, verified line 69) — All `gh api .../environments/release` calls in the doc correctly use the name. The id on line 69 is informational — fine — but its prominence in the doc invites a future maintainer to paste it into an id-keyed call that doesn't exist. Minor.

- **`reviewers: [{type: User, id: 132626034}]` hard-codes a numeric user id in the restore block.** (drift, verified lines 75, 109) — If `armelhbobdad` ever transfers the repo, is renamed, or leaves, the id lookup is silently wrong. GitHub's environment API accepts user id but not login in this field. The doc does not explain how to re-derive the id (`gh api users/<login> --jq .id`).

- **Both heredocs use `'JSON'` (single-quoted) which is correct for the JSON payloads — but both would break if a maintainer edits in a `$variable` expecting interpolation.** (encoding, verified lines 105, 118, 131) — Not a defect as-shipped; a latent hazard for future edits. Worth a one-line "note: `'JSON'` disables `$var` expansion — use `\"JSON\"` if you need interpolation."

- **"Merge blocked until all seven `quality.yaml` checks pass" — but `quality.yaml` also has a `workflow_dispatch` trigger.** (boundary, verified against `.github/workflows/quality.yaml:16–19`) — The ruleset requires the check contexts to report on the PR head. If a check is only reported via `workflow_dispatch` (not `pull_request`), it never associates with the PR and the required-checks gate blocks forever. Since the file has both triggers with `pull_request: branches: ["**"]`, we're fine today — but the doc does not state this invariant, so a future maintainer who splits jobs across workflows or changes the triggers can silently deadlock PRs.

It's correct today but the doc does not link to GitHub's own definition of `bypass_mode`, so if semantics change (GitHub has quietly renamed ruleset fields twice) the guarantee silently breaks.

The doc's claim matches reality today. But the inline "currently a no-op" paragraph will silently become wrong the moment someone adds the file without updating this doc.

Minor.

GitHub periodically renames runner images (`ubuntu-latest` currently points to 24.04; it was 22.04 a year ago). The required-status-check names are literal strings reported by the runner — if a future matrix change moves to `ubuntu-24.04` explicitly, the check names the ruleset requires (`validate (ubuntu-latest)`) will no longer appear. Not today's problem, but the doc invites it.

- **"`release.yaml` workflow (Story 3.1)" is referenced as a future file, hard-coded as the npm trusted-publisher workflow filename.** (drift, verified lines 42, 84) — npm trusted publishing binds on the workflow filename. If Story 3.1 names the file anything other than `release.yaml` (`publish.yaml`, `npm-release.yaml`, etc.), the npm side will 404 and the four-field invariant at line 84 is violated. The doc states this correctly as a constraint — but since the file does not yet exist, there's nothing preventing the author of Story 3.1 from picking a different name. A `TODO(Story 3.1): filename must be release.yaml` comment in a placeholder file would catch this earlier.

Acceptable for an internal maintainer doc, but worth at least a link to `_bmad-output/` or wherever these live.

- **Markdown tables (lines 18–24 and 71–78) use pipe characters inside some cells (`{ main }`, `custom_branch_policies: true`, `list: main only`).** (encoding, verified lines 76–78) — The pipes are not escaped. Some Markdown renderers (strict CommonMark tables) will treat an unescaped `|` inside a cell as a column separator and break the table layout. Rendered on GitHub today the table survives because GitHub's GFM is lenient, but a Starlight/other renderer may not be.

- **The "no secrets on release env" invariant is stated but not automatically enforced.** (drift, verified line 77, 82) — "Environment-scoped secrets: 0 (invariant)". Nothing in the repo asserts this. A maintainer (or a helpful bot) could add a secret and break the OIDC trusted-publisher story without any CI signal. The doc says "re-audit" — a scripted assertion (a CI step that calls `gh api .../environments/release/secrets` and fails if `total_count > 0`) would turn the invariant into a check.

- **`prevent_self_review: false` flips-on-second-reviewer rule is purely prose.** (drift, verified line 80) — There is no reminder anywhere in the repo that flips this when a second reviewer is added to the environment. The warning "do not leave it loose by inertia" relies on the maintainer remembering. If a second reviewer is added via the GitHub UI, nothing prompts an update to `prevent_self_review`.

- **Diff claims `docs/RELEASING.md` is a new file (`new file mode`) and I verified this matches the current state — but commit history shows `1de3a2d docs(releasing): document hardened main ruleset` already landed.** (drift, verified `git log`) — The "new file" claim in the diff is accurate for this PR against `dev`, but commit `1de3a2d` on the current branch already touched release docs. If the PR is rebased against a main that already contains a different `RELEASING.md` (from a parallel branch), the "new file" claim becomes a merge conflict. Low probability but worth knowing.

@@ -0,0 +1,149 @@
+---
+title: Releasing SKF
+description: Maintainer reference for the release pipeline — branch protection, release workflow, and rollback procedures.
+---
+
+This document records the configuration that gates releases of `bmad-module-skill-forge`. It exists so a future maintainer (including future-you) can audit, restore, or extend the pipeline without reverse-engineering GitHub settings.
+
+For background on GitHub rulesets vs legacy branch protection, see the [GitHub ruleset docs](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/configuring-branches-and-merges-in-your-repository/managing-rulesets/about-rulesets).
+
+## Branch Protection on `main`
+
+`main` is gated by a **GitHub repository ruleset** (not legacy branch protection). The legacy "Settings → Branches → Branch protection rules" surface returns 404 for this repo.
+
+**Ruleset:** `Default` — id `13855503` — applies to `~DEFAULT_BRANCH` (currently `main`) — enforcement `active`.
+
+**Active rules (5):**
+
+| Rule                     | Effect                                                                                                                     |
+|--------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
+| `deletion`               | Branch cannot be deleted.                                                                                                  |
+| `non_fast_forward`       | Force-push blocked.                                                                                                        |
+| `pull_request`           | Requires ≥ 1 approving review; `require_code_owner_review: true` (see CODEOWNERS note below); merge/squash/rebase allowed. |
+| `code_quality`           | Blocks merge on `severity: errors` from GitHub code-quality checks.                                                        |
+| `required_status_checks` | Merge blocked until all seven `quality.yaml` checks pass (names below).                                                    |
+
+**Required status checks (7):** sourced from `.github/workflows/quality.yaml` job keys. Matrix jobs expand to `jobname (matrix-value)`:
+
+- `prettier`
+- `eslint`
+- `markdownlint`
+- `validate (ubuntu-latest)`
+- `validate (windows-latest)`
+- `python (ubuntu-latest)`
+- `python (windows-latest)`
+
+**Coupling with `quality.yaml`:** if that workflow renames a job or changes the `strategy.matrix.os` for `validate` or `python`, the ruleset's `required_status_checks` list must be updated in lock-step — otherwise merges to `main` will either block on a check name that no longer reports, or silently pass without the renamed check. Update both in the same PR.
+
+**`strict_required_status_checks_policy: false`** — PR branches are not forced to be up-to-date with `main` before merging. This avoids constant rebases on a low-traffic repo. Flip to `true` if concurrent merges start producing logical conflicts the checks can't catch.
+
+**CODEOWNERS note:** the `pull_request` rule has `require_code_owner_review: true`, but no `.github/CODEOWNERS` file exists in the repo today. GitHub treats the code-owner requirement as vacuously satisfied when the file is absent, so this setting is currently a no-op — the only active review gate is `required_approving_review_count: 1`. If a CODEOWNERS file is added later, make sure the listed owners can actually approve PRs from other authors (GitHub disallows self-review as a code owner, which would deadlock solo-maintainer PRs).
+
+**Bypass actors:** `RepositoryRole` actor_id=5 (Admin), `bypass_mode: pull_request`. Admins can bypass the ruleset **only via a pull request**, never via direct push. This preserves `non_fast_forward` and the required-checks gate for the `github-actions[bot]` account that the future `release.yaml` workflow (Story 3.1) will use to push tags and commits to `main`. **Do not add a bot-specific bypass**; it would defeat the whole purpose of this ruleset.
+
+### Inspect current state
+
+```bash
+gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets/13855503
+```
+
+### Restore from a saved baseline
+
+A JSON baseline captured before any change can be replayed via:
+
+```bash
+# Extract rules + bypass_actors from a saved baseline.json
+jq '{rules, bypass_actors}' baseline.json > /tmp/restore.json
+
+gh api --method PUT \
+  repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets/13855503 \
+  --input /tmp/restore.json
+```
+
+The GitHub ruleset API uses `PUT` (not `PATCH`) for updates, and the `rules` array is replaced wholesale — it is not merged server-side. Always fetch current state, modify the in-memory copy, and `PUT` the complete list.
+
+## Release Environment
+
+The publish job is gated by a **GitHub [deployment environment](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/deployment/targeting-different-environments/using-environments-for-deployment) with a required-reviewer rule** (not by workflow logic). A job declaring `environment: release` pauses until a listed reviewer clicks "Approve and deploy" in the Actions UI.
+
+**Environment:** `release` — id `14347249917` — created 2026-04-20.
+
+| Setting                      | Value                                               |
+|------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|
+| `wait_timer`                 | `0` (no artificial delay; approval is the only gate) |
+| `prevent_self_review`        | `false` — see rationale below                       |
+| `reviewers`                  | `armelhbobdad` (user id `132626034`), 1 approver    |
+| `deployment_branch_policy`   | `custom_branch_policies: true`, list: `main` only   |
+| Environment-scoped secrets   | `0` (invariant — see `NPM_TOKEN` note below)        |
+| Cost                         | `$0` on public-repo tier (environments, required reviewers, and branch policies are [free for public repositories](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/deployment/targeting-different-environments/using-environments-for-deployment#about-environments)) |
+
+**`prevent_self_review: false` — correctness constraint, not a loosened control.** Solo-maintainer setups cannot self-approve when this is `true`, so the gate would deadlock on any maintainer-triggered publish. The value flips to `true` the moment a second reviewer joins — do not leave it loose by inertia.
+
+**`NPM_TOKEN` is NOT scoped to this environment.** It remains at repo-level secrets during the OIDC transition and is removed entirely post-v1.0.0 (see Story 6.3). The invariant: the `release` env must have zero secrets. If a future change scopes any secret here, re-audit whether the OIDC trusted-publisher path is still in force.
+
+**Coupling with npm trusted publishing (Story 1.3).** The npm trusted publisher binds on four fields — `organization=armelhbobdad`, `repository=bmad-module-skill-forge`, `workflow filename=release.yaml`, `environment=release`. The environment name above is load-bearing: any rename here must be accompanied by a matching npm-side update in the same change, or the next publish returns 404.
+
+### Inspect current state
+
+```bash
+# Environment itself (reviewers, branch-policy shape, prevent_self_review)
+gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release
+
+# Allowed deployment branches (expect: one entry, name "main")
+gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies
+
+# Environment-scoped secrets (expect: zero)
+gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/secrets
+```
+
+### Restore / re-apply
+
+Two-call pattern — the environment and its branch-policy list are separate resources. Creating the environment alone with `custom_branch_policies: true` leaves the allow-list empty, which rejects every dispatch; the second call is mandatory.
+
+```bash
+# (1) Create or update the environment with the required-reviewer gate
+gh api --method PUT repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release --input - <<'JSON'
+{
+  "wait_timer": 0,
+  "prevent_self_review": false,
+  "reviewers": [{ "type": "User", "id": 132626034 }],
+  "deployment_branch_policy": {
+    "protected_branches": false,
+    "custom_branch_policies": true
+  }
+}
+JSON
+
+# (2) Add main to the allowed deployment branches
+gh api --method POST repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies --input - <<'JSON'
+{ "name": "main" }
+JSON
+```
+
+### Temporarily allowing a feature branch
+
+A release workflow that declares `environment: release` will be rejected from any branch not on the allow-list. For legitimate validation cuts from a feature branch (Story 3.2's alpha cut is the canonical case), widen the allow-list for the duration of the test and tighten it back immediately:
+
+```bash
+# Allow the feature branch (capture the returned policy id)
+gh api --method POST \
+  repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies \
+  --input - <<'JSON'
+{ "name": "feat/my-validation-branch" }
+JSON
+# → { "id": <POLICY_ID>, "name": "feat/my-validation-branch", ... }
+
+# ... run the validation cut, approve via the reviewer UI, confirm publish ...
+
+# Revoke immediately — the allow-list must return to { main } before leaving the session
+gh api --method DELETE \
+  repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies/<POLICY_ID>
+```
+
+Leaving a feature branch on the allow-list is an unguarded hole — any later `workflow_dispatch` from that branch would be able to reach the publish path. Treat the revoke as the last step of the validation, not a follow-up.
+
+<!-- Rollback Playbook — added in Story 4.1 -->
+
+## Rollback Playbook
+
+_To be authored in Story 4.1 — rollback playbook covering scenarios such as failed publish, tag mismatch, bad `latest` dist-tag, OIDC auth failure, and post-publish security issue._
```

The allow-list then carries the feature branch indefinitely — which the doc itself calls "an unguarded hole." A `POLICY_ID=$(gh api ... --jq '.id')` pattern would make the revoke mechanically safe.

- **Revoke step is described as "the last step of the validation, not a follow-up" but the shell snippet has no trap/cleanup on failure between the two.** (ordering, verified lines 136–140) — If the validation cut between step 1 (add branch) and the revoke (delete branch) errors out, crashes the shell, or the maintainer Ctrl-C's, the feature branch remains on the allow-list. A `trap 'gh api --method DELETE ...' EXIT` pattern would close this gap; the doc's prose warning alone does not.

- **`gh api` calls assume authentication + scopes the doc never states.** (precondition, verified entire file) — Ruleset writes require `admin:repo` on the authenticating token; environment writes require `repo` scope + admin role. `gh auth status` is never mentioned. A maintainer with a read-only token will see opaque 403s from every command in the doc.

- **`jq` dependency in the "Restore from a saved baseline" block is not stated as a precondition.** (precondition, verified line 56) — `jq '{rules, bypass_actors}' baseline.json` assumes `jq` is installed. On a fresh macOS or Windows maintainer machine this may not be true. `gh api --jq` would keep the dep inside `gh`.

- **The `jq '{rules, bypass_actors}'` extraction drops `conditions`, `enforcement`, `name`, `target`, and `bypass_actors` shape details.** (drift/encoding, verified line 56) — A PUT that only contains `{rules, bypass_actors}` will succeed, but the ruleset name, target (`~DEFAULT_BRANCH`), and enforcement (`active`) fields are not in the payload. GitHub's PUT replaces the entire resource — if the server requires those fields (it does, per the API ref), this call will 422. If it doesn't, the ruleset may silently revert `enforcement` to the default or lose its target. The doc's own later paragraph says "the `rules` array is replaced wholesale — it is not merged server-side" but the restore snippet only restores rules, not the surrounding configuration.

- **Baseline.json source is unspecified.** (precondition, verified line 52–56) — "A JSON baseline captured before any change" — no guidance on when/how to capture it, or where it lives. If a maintainer needs this during an incident and no baseline exists, the whole section is dead. The repo has no checked-in baseline and no scripted capture, so the restore path is aspirational.

- **Ruleset id `13855503` and environment id `14347249917` are hard-coded — if either is deleted and recreated, the id changes and every `gh api .../rulesets/13855503` in the doc breaks.** (drift, verified lines 14, 47, 59–60, 69) — The doc has no fallback instruction like "if the id has changed, find it with `gh api repos/.../rulesets --jq '.[] | select(.name==\"Default\") | .id'`." A future recreate will leave the maintainer with a 404 and no remediation path.

- **`release` environment id `14347249917` is hard-coded, but the environment API is keyed by *name*, not id.** (drift, verified line 69) — All `gh api .../environments/release` calls in the doc correctly use the name. The id on line 69 is informational — fine — but its prominence in the doc invites a future maintainer to paste it into an id-keyed call that doesn't exist. Minor.

- **`reviewers: [{type: User, id: 132626034}]` hard-codes a numeric user id in the restore block.** (drift, verified lines 75, 109) — If `armelhbobdad` ever transfers the repo, is renamed, or leaves, the id lookup is silently wrong. GitHub's environment API accepts user id but not login in this field. The doc does not explain how to re-derive the id (`gh api users/<login> --jq .id`).

- **Both heredocs use `'JSON'` (single-quoted) which is correct for the JSON payloads — but both would break if a maintainer edits in a `$variable` expecting interpolation.** (encoding, verified lines 105, 118, 131) — Not a defect as-shipped; a latent hazard for future edits. Worth a one-line "note: `'JSON'` disables `$var` expansion — use `\"JSON\"` if you need interpolation."

- **"Merge blocked until all seven `quality.yaml` checks pass" — but `quality.yaml` also has a `workflow_dispatch` trigger.** (boundary, verified against `.github/workflows/quality.yaml:16–19`) — The ruleset requires the check contexts to report on the PR head. If a check is only reported via `workflow_dispatch` (not `pull_request`), it never associates with the PR and the required-checks gate blocks forever. Since the file has both triggers with `pull_request: branches: ["**"]`, we're fine today — but the doc does not state this invariant, so a future maintainer who splits jobs across workflows or changes the triggers can silently deadlock PRs.

- **"Admins can bypass the ruleset only via a pull request" assumes GitHub's `bypass_mode: pull_request` semantics stay what they are.** (drift, verified line 42) — This is a behavioral claim about GitHub's rulesets API. It's correct today but the doc does not link to GitHub's own definition of `bypass_mode`, so if semantics change (GitHub has quietly renamed ruleset fields twice) the guarantee silently breaks.

- **The doc says no `.github/CODEOWNERS` file exists; verified — but the diff states this inline rather than in a check.** (drift, verified) — I confirmed no `CODEOWNERS` exists at `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/.github/CODEOWNERS`, `/CODEOWNERS`, or `/docs/CODEOWNERS`. The doc's claim matches reality today. But the inline "currently a no-op" paragraph will silently become wrong the moment someone adds the file without updating this doc.

- **The `docs/RELEASING.md` page uses a Starlight-compatible frontmatter (`title`, `description`) but the nearby `docs/index.md` uses Starlight's `hero.actions` shape.** (drift, suspected) — If this site is built with Starlight, adding a page without a sidebar entry may leave it unreachable from site nav. Verified `docs/_data/pinned.yaml` exists; I did not read it, but RELEASING.md is not listed alongside the other docs in any nav file I spotted. A reader landing on the site will not find the page unless they know the URL.

- **`README.md` anchor `## Release Process` is the only README pointer to RELEASING.md, added adjacent to `CONTRIBUTING.md`.** (boundary, verified `README.md:178–180`) — CONTRIBUTING.md likely belongs in the same vicinity; if a future README reshuffle moves "Release Process" or CONTRIBUTING, the relative placement assumption (maintainer doc after contributor doc) may drift. Minor.

- **No word count / character-count drift check for the matrix OS values.** (drift, verified lines 31–34 vs `quality.yaml:83,126`) — The doc hard-codes `ubuntu-latest` and `windows-latest`. GitHub periodically renames runner images (`ubuntu-latest` currently points to 24.04; it was 22.04 a year ago). The required-status-check names are literal strings reported by the runner — if a future matrix change moves to `ubuntu-24.04` explicitly, the check names the ruleset requires (`validate (ubuntu-latest)`) will no longer appear. Not today's problem, but the doc invites it.

- **"`release.yaml` workflow (Story 3.1)" is referenced as a future file, hard-coded as the npm trusted-publisher workflow filename.** (drift, verified lines 42, 84) — npm trusted publishing binds on the workflow filename. If Story 3.1 names the file anything other than `release.yaml` (`publish.yaml`, `npm-release.yaml`, etc.), the npm side will 404 and the four-field invariant at line 84 is violated. The doc states this correctly as a constraint — but since the file does not yet exist, there's nothing preventing the author of Story 3.1 from picking a different name. A `TODO(Story 3.1): filename must be release.yaml` comment in a placeholder file would catch this earlier.

- **"Story 4.1", "Story 6.3", "Story 1.3", "Story 3.2", "Story 3.1" references assume a story-tracking system the doc does not link.** (precondition, verified lines 42, 82, 84, 125, 147–149) — A reader outside the maintainer's head cannot resolve these. Acceptable for an internal maintainer doc, but worth at least a link to `_bmad-output/` or wherever these live.

- **Markdown tables (lines 18–24 and 71–78) use pipe characters inside some cells (`{ main }`, `custom_branch_policies: true`, `list: main only`).** (encoding, verified lines 76–78) — The pipes are not escaped. Some Markdown renderers (strict CommonMark tables) will treat an unescaped `|` inside a cell as a column separator and break the table layout. Rendered on GitHub today the table survives because GitHub's GFM is lenient, but a Starlight/other renderer may not be.

- **The "no secrets on release env" invariant is stated but not automatically enforced.** (drift, verified line 77, 82) — "Environment-scoped secrets: 0 (invariant)". Nothing in the repo asserts this. A maintainer (or a helpful bot) could add a secret and break the OIDC trusted-publisher story without any CI signal. The doc says "re-audit" — a scripted assertion (a CI step that calls `gh api .../environments/release/secrets` and fails if `total_count > 0`) would turn the invariant into a check.

- **`prevent_self_review: false` flips-on-second-reviewer rule is purely prose.** (drift, verified line 80) — There is no reminder anywhere in the repo that flips this when a second reviewer is added to the environment. The warning "do not leave it loose by inertia" relies on the maintainer remembering. If a second reviewer is added via the GitHub UI, nothing prompts an update to `prevent_self_review`.

- **Diff claims `docs/RELEASING.md` is a new file (`new file mode`) and I verified this matches the current state — but commit history shows `1de3a2d docs(releasing): document hardened main ruleset` already landed.** (drift, verified `git log`) — The "new file" claim in the diff is accurate for this PR against `dev`, but commit `1de3a2d` on the current branch already touched release docs. If the PR is rebased against a main that already contains a different `RELEASING.md` (from a parallel branch), the "new file" claim becomes a merge conflict. Low probability but worth knowing.

---

# Step 3: Triage

## RULES

- YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
- Be precise. When uncertain between categories, prefer the more conservative classification.

## INSTRUCTIONS

1. **Normalize** findings into a common format. Expected input formats:
   - Adversarial (Blind Hunter): markdown list of descriptions
   - Edge Case Hunter: JSON array with `location`, `trigger_condition`, `guard_snippet`, `potential_consequence` fields
   - Acceptance Auditor: markdown list with title, AC/constraint reference, and evidence

   If a layer's output does not match its expected format, attempt best-effort parsing. Note any parsing issues for the user.

   Convert all to a unified list where each finding has:
   - `id` -- sequential integer
   - `source` -- `blind`, `edge`, `auditor`, or merged sources (e.g., `blind+edge`)
   - `title` -- one-line summary
   - `detail` -- full description
   - `location` -- file and line reference (if available)

2. **Deduplicate.** If two or more findings describe the same issue, merge them into one:
   - Use the most specific finding as the base (prefer edge-case JSON with location over adversarial prose).
   - Append any unique detail, reasoning, or location references from the other finding(s) into the surviving `detail` field.
   - Set `source` to the merged sources (e.g., `blind+edge`).

3. **Classify** each finding into exactly one bucket:
   - **decision_needed** -- There is an ambiguous choice that requires human input. The code cannot be correctly patched without knowing the user's intent. Only possible if `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
   - **patch** -- Code issue that is fixable without human input. The correct fix is unambiguous.
   - **defer** -- Pre-existing issue not caused by the current change. Real but not actionable now.
   - **dismiss** -- Noise, false positive, or handled elsewhere.

   If `{review_mode}` = `"no-spec"` and a finding would otherwise be `decision_needed`, reclassify it as `patch` (if the fix is unambiguous) or `defer` (if not).

4. **Drop** all `dismiss` findings. Record the dismiss count for the summary.

5. If `{failed_layers}` is non-empty, report which layers failed before announcing results. If zero findings remain after dropping dismissed AND `{failed_layers}` is non-empty, warn the user that the review may be incomplete rather than announcing a clean review.

6. If zero findings remain after triage (all rejected or none raised): state "✅ Clean review — all layers passed." (Step 3 already warned if any review layers failed via `{failed_layers}`.)


## NEXT

Read fully and follow `./step-04-present.md`

Author:     armel <armelhbobdad@gmail.com>
AuthorDate: Mon Apr 20 13:50:21 2026 +0400
Commit:     armel <armelhbobdad@gmail.com>
CommitDate: Mon Apr 20 13:50:21 2026 +0400

    feat(releasing): create release GitHub environment with required-reviewer gate
    
    Adds the release publish gate that Story 3.1's release.yaml will bind to
    via `environment: release`, and that Story 1.3's npm trusted publisher
    registration will reference character-for-character.
    
    Remote GitHub config (no file diff — captured in docs/RELEASING.md):
    - Environment `release` (id 14347249917) created on armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge
    - Required reviewer: armelhbobdad (user id 132626034), 1 approver
    - prevent_self_review: false (solo-maintainer — true would deadlock)
    - deployment_branch_policy: custom_branch_policies=true, allowed: [main]
    - Environment-scoped secrets: 0 (NPM_TOKEN remains repo-scoped only,
      removed entirely in Story 6.3)
    
    Dry-run evidence (AC #4):
    - Run: https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/actions/runs/24659654845
    - Waiting at 2026-04-20T09:43:40Z; job `gated` started 09:46:03Z after
      maintainer approval; completed 09:46:06Z success. ~2m 23s gap proves
      the gate held until approval, not a timing coincidence.
    - Throwaway branch (throwaway/env-gate-test) and its temporary
      deployment-branch-policy entry deleted; only {main} remains.
    
    Note on trigger type: the task originally specified workflow_dispatch,
    but that trigger requires the workflow file on the default branch,
    which would have forced merging a throwaway file into main. Switched
    to `on: push: branches: [throwaway/env-gate-test]` — same gate engages
    (the `environment: release` binding is what triggers the reviewer
    rule), no main pollution.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
    Entire-Checkpoint: 9a86697a3008

4	# - Prettier (formatting)

file.content:
name: Quality & Validation

# Runs comprehensive quality checks on all PRs:
# - Prettier (formatting)
# - ESLint (linting)
# - markdownlint (markdown quality)
# - Schema validation (YAML structure)
# - Agent schema tests (fixture-based validation)
# - Installation component tests (CLI + compilation)
# - Workflow state consistency tests
# - Knowledge base tests
# - Skill structure validation
# - File reference validation
# - Python script tests (pytest via uv)

"on":
  pull_request:
    branches: ["**"]
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  prettier:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Setup Node
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version-file: ".nvmrc"
          cache: "npm"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: Prettier format check
        run: npm run format:check

  eslint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Setup Node
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version-file: ".nvmrc"
          cache: "npm"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: ESLint
        run: npm run lint

  markdownlint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Setup Node
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version-file: ".nvmrc"
          cache: "npm"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: markdownlint
        run: npm run lint:md

  validate:
    # Matrix Linux + Windows to catch platform-divergence bugs (path handling,
    # line endings, shell scripts). macOS skipped: POSIX-equivalent to Linux
    # for SKF's Node + Python surface area — add only if a Mac-only bug surfaces.
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest, windows-latest]
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Setup Node
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version-file: ".nvmrc"
          cache: "npm"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: Validate YAML schemas
        run: npm run validate:schemas

      - name: Run agent schema validation tests
        run: npm run test:schemas

      - name: Test installation components
        run: npm run test:install

      - name: Test CLI integration
        run: npm run test:cli

      - name: Test workflow state consistency
        run: npm run test:workflow

      - name: Test knowledge base
        run: npm run test:knowledge

      - name: Validate skills structure
        run: npm run validate:skills

      - name: Validate file references
        run: npm run validate:refs

  python:
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest, windows-latest]
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install uv
        uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v6
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"

      - name: Setup Node
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version-file: ".nvmrc"
          cache: "npm"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: Run Python tests
        run: npm run test:python

content.content: 1 --- 2 deferred_work_file: '{implementation_artifacts}/deferred-work.md' 3 --- 4 5 # Step 4: Present and Act 6 7 ## RULES 8 9 - YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}` 10 - When `{spec_file}` is set, always write findings to the story file before offering action choices. 11 - `decision-needed` findings must be resolved before handling `patch` findings. 12 13 ## INSTRUCTIONS 14 15 ### 1. Clean review shortcut 16 17 If zero findings remain after triage (all dismissed or none raised): state that and proceed to section 6 (Sprint Status Update). 18 19 ### 2. Write findings to the story file 20 21 If `{spec_file}` exists and contains a Tasks/Subtasks section, append a `### Review Findings` subsection. Write all findings in this order: 22 23 1. **`decision-needed`** findings (unchecked): 24 `- [ ] [Review][Decision] — <Detail>` 25 26 2. **`patch`** findings (unchecked): 27 `- [ ] [Review][Patch] <Title> [<file>:<line>]` 28 29 3. **`defer`** findings (checked off, marked deferred): 30 `- [x] [Review][Defer] <Title> [<file>:<line>] — deferred, pre-existing` 31 32 Also append each `defer` finding to `{deferred_work_file}` under a heading `## Deferred from: code review ({date})`. If `{spec_file}` is set, include its basename in the heading (e.g., `code review of story-3.3 (2026-03-18)`). One bullet per finding with description. 33 34 ### 3. Present summary 35 36 Announce what was written: 37 38 > **Code review complete.** <D> `decision-needed`, <P> `patch`, <W> `defer`, <R> dismissed as noise. 39 40 If `{spec_file}` is set, add: `Findings written to the review findings section in {spec_file}.` 41 Otherwise add: `Findings are listed above. No story file was provided, so nothing was persisted.` 42 43 ### 4. Resolve decision-needed findings 44 45 If `decision_needed` findings exist, present each one with its detail and the options available. The user must decide — the correct fix is ambiguous without their input. Walk through each finding (or batch related ones) and get the user's call. Once resolved, each becomes a `patch`, `defer`, or is dismissed. 46 47 If the user chooses to defer, ask: Quick one-line reason for deferring this item? (helps future reviews): — then append that reason to both the story file bullet and the `{deferred_work_file}` entry. 48 49 **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. 50 51 ### 5. Handle `patch` findings 52 53 If `patch` findings exist (including any resolved from step 4), HALT. Ask the user: 54 55 If `{spec_file}` is set, present all three options (if >3 `patch` findings exist, also show option 0): 56 57 > **How would you like to handle the <Z> `patch` findings?** 58 > 0. **Batch-apply all** — automatically fix every non-controversial patch (recommended when there are many) 59 > 1. **Fix them automatically** — I will apply fixes now 60 > 2. **Leave as action items** — they are already in the story file 61 > 3. **Walk through each** — let me show details before deciding 62 63 If `{spec_file}` is **not** set, present only options 1 and 3 (omit option 2 — findings were not written to a file). If >3 `patch` findings exist, also show option 0: 64 65 > **How would you like to handle the <Z> `patch` findings?** 66 > 0. **Batch-apply all** — automatically fix every non-controversial patch (recommended when there are many) 67 > 1. **Fix them automatically** — I will apply fixes now 68 > 2. **Walk through each** — let me show details before deciding 69 70 **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. 71 72 - **Option 0** (only when >3 findings): Apply all non-controversial patches without per-finding confirmation. Skip any finding that requires judgment. Present a summary of changes made and any skipped findings. 73 - **Option 1**: Apply each fix. After all patches are applied, present a summary of changes made. If `{spec_file}` is set, check off the items in the story file. 74 - **Option 2** (only when `{spec_file}` is set): Done — findings are already written to the story. 75 - **Walk through each**: Present each finding with full detail, diff context, and suggested fix. After walkthrough, re-offer the applicable options above. 76 77 **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. 78 79 **✅ Code review actions complete** 80 81 - Decision-needed resolved: <D> 82 - Patches handled: <P> 83 - Deferred: <W> 84 - Dismissed: <R> 85 86 ### 6. Update story status and sync sprint tracking 87 88 Skip this section if `{spec_file}` is not set. 89 90 #### Determine new status based on review outcome 91 92 - If all `decision-needed` and `patch` findings were resolved (fixed or dismissed) AND no unresolved HIGH/MEDIUM issues remain: set `{new_status}` = `done`. Update the story file Status section to `done`. 93 - If `patch` findings were left as action items, or unresolved issues remain: set `{new_status}` = `in-progress`. Update the story file Status section to `in-progress`. 94 95 Save the story file. 96 97 #### Sync sprint-status.yaml 98 99 If `{story_key}` is not set, skip this subsection and note that sprint status was not synced because no story key was available. 100 101 If `{sprint_status}` file exists: 102 103 1. Load the FULL `{sprint_status}` file. 104 2. Find the `development_status` entry matching `{story_key}`. 105 3. If found: update `development_status[{story_key}]` to `{new_status}`. Update `last_updated` to current date. Save the file, preserving ALL comments and structure including STATUS DEFINITIONS. 106 4. If `{story_key}` not found in sprint status: warn the user that the story file was updated but sprint-status sync failed. 107 108 If `{sprint_status}` file does not exist, note that story status was updated in the story file only. 109 110 #### Completion summary 111 112 > **Review Complete!** 113 > 114 > **Story Status:** `{new_status}` 115 > **Issues Fixed:** <fixed_count> 116 > **Action Items Created:** <action_count> 117 > **Deferred:** <W> 118 > **Dismissed:** <R> 119 120 ### 7. Next steps 121 122 Present the user with follow-up options: 123 124 > **What would you like to do next?** 125 > 1. **Start the next story** — run `dev-story` to pick up the next `ready-for-dev` story 126 > 2. **Re-run code review** — address findings and review again 127 > 3. **Done** — end the workflow 128 129 **HALT** — I am waiting for your choice. Do not proceed until the user selects an option. 130

content.text: Writing findings to the story file now.

content.content:
55	
56	- [x] **Task 6 — Commit with auditable evidence** (AC: all)
57	  - [x] Stage `docs/RELEASING.md` only; no code, no workflow YAML (the dry-run workflow was a throwaway and is deleted by now)
58	  - [x] Commit message SHALL include: the dry-run workflow run URL from Task 3, the approval-event timestamp, the maintainer user id used, and a one-line confirmation that `NPM_TOKEN` remains repo-scoped only
59	  - [x] Use conventional-commit format: `feat(releasing): create release GitHub environment with required-reviewer gate`
60	
61	## Dev Notes
62	
63	### What this story actually is
64	
65	This is a **GitHub configuration change + one doc-file extension**, not a code change. The work lives in the GitHub Environments API (preferred) or Repo Settings → Environments UI (fallback). No workflow YAML lands on `main` from this story — the dry-run workflow in Task 3 is a throwaway that gets deleted before the final commit.
66	
67	**Scope discipline — what NOT to do here:**
68	- Do NOT author `release.yaml` — that is Story 3.1.
69	- Do NOT register the npm trusted publisher — that is Story 1.3 (this story only produces the environment name it will bind to).
70	- Do NOT add or move the `NPM_TOKEN` secret — it stays at repo level until Story 6.3.
71	- Do NOT add a `github-actions[bot]` reviewer or any bot-specific bypass — the gate is specifically for human approval (consistent with Story 1.1's posture on bot bypass).
72	
73	### Starting state — assumed 2026-04-20
74	
75	- No environments exist on the repo (to be confirmed by Task 1's list call).
76	- Story 1.1 already hardened `main` branch protection via ruleset `13855503` and created `docs/RELEASING.md` with a "Branch Protection on `main`" section + a `<!-- Rollback Playbook — added in Story 4.1 -->` placeholder.
77	- `NPM_TOKEN` exists at repo-level secrets (used today by legacy `publish.yaml`/`manual-release.yaml`; will be removed in Story 6.3).
78	
79	### GitHub Environments API reference
80	
81	**Create or update an environment:**
82	```
83	PUT /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/{environment_name}
84	```
85	Returns 200 on update of an existing env, 201 on create. Idempotent.
86	
87	**Add a custom branch policy (required after creating the env with `custom_branch_policies: true`):**
88	```
89	POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/{environment_name}/deployment-branch-policies
90	Body: { "name": "main" }
91	```
92	
93	**List environment secrets (used to verify NPM_TOKEN is NOT scoped here):**
94	```
95	GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/{environment_name}/secrets
96	```
97	
98	**List environments:**
99	```
100	GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments
101	```
102	
103	All calls require a `repo`-scoped token; `gh auth status` should already satisfy this for the maintainer account.
104	
105	### Known-good JSON payloads
106	
107	**Payload A — create `release` environment with required reviewer:**
108	
109	```json
110	{
111	  "wait_timer": 0,
112	  "prevent_self_review": false,
113	  "reviewers": [
114	    { "type": "User", "id": <REPLACE_WITH_MAINTAINER_USER_ID> }
115	  ],
116	  "deployment_branch_policy": {
117	    "protected_branches": false,
118	    "custom_branch_policies": true
119	  }
120	}
121	```
122	
123	Field notes:
124	- `wait_timer: 0` — no artificial delay; approval is the only gate.
125	- `prevent_self_review: false` — **critical for solo-maintainer.** If set to `true`, the maintainer who triggers the workflow cannot approve it, which means no one can, which means the gate deadlocks. Revisit only when a second reviewer joins.
126	- `reviewers` — `type` must be exactly `"User"` or `"Team"`. `id` is the numeric user/team id, not the username. Fetch with `gh api users/armelhbobdad --jq .id`.
127	- `deployment_branch_policy: protected_branches: false` + `custom_branch_policies: true` — these two booleans are mutually exclusive; the combination here enables the custom-list branch scope (populated by the second call below). Using `protected_branches: true` would also work since `main` is protected, but `custom_branch_policies: true` gives tighter control and matches the AC #5 language.
128	
129	**Payload B — add `main` to the allowed deployment branches (second call, after Payload A):**
130	
131	```json
132	{ "name": "main" }
133	```
134

- [x] **Task 6 — Commit with auditable evidence** (AC: all)
  - [x] Stage `docs/RELEASING.md` only; no code, no workflow YAML (the dry-run workflow was a throwaway and is deleted by now)
  - [x] Commit message SHALL include: the dry-run workflow run URL from Task 3, the approval-event timestamp, the maintainer user id used, and a one-line confirmation that `NPM_TOKEN` remains repo-scoped only
  - [x] Use conventional-commit format: `feat(releasing): create release GitHub environment with required-reviewer gate`

## Dev Notes

### What this story actually is

This is a **GitHub configuration change + one doc-file extension**, not a code change. The work lives in the GitHub Environments API (preferred) or Repo Settings → Environments UI (fallback). No workflow YAML lands on `main` from this story — the dry-run workflow in Task 3 is a throwaway that gets deleted before the final commit.

**Scope discipline — what NOT to do here:**
- Do NOT author `release.yaml` — that is Story 3.1.
- Do NOT register the npm trusted publisher — that is Story 1.3 (this story only produces the environment name it will bind to).
- Do NOT add or move the `NPM_TOKEN` secret — it stays at repo level until Story 6.3.
- Do NOT add a `github-actions[bot]` reviewer or any bot-specific bypass — the gate is specifically for human approval (consistent with Story 1.1's posture on bot bypass).

### Starting state — assumed 2026-04-20

- No environments exist on the repo (to be confirmed by Task 1's list call).
- Story 1.1 already hardened `main` branch protection via ruleset `13855503` and created `docs/RELEASING.md` with a "Branch Protection on `main`" section + a `<!-- Rollback Playbook — added in Story 4.1 -->` placeholder.
- `NPM_TOKEN` exists at repo-level secrets (used today by legacy `publish.yaml`/`manual-release.yaml`; will be removed in Story 6.3).

### GitHub Environments API reference

**Create or update an environment:**
```
PUT /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/{environment_name}
```
Returns 200 on update of an existing env, 201 on create. Idempotent.

**Add a custom branch policy (required after creating the env with `custom_branch_policies: true`):**
```
POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/{environment_name}/deployment-branch-policies
Body: { "name": "main" }
```

**List environment secrets (used to verify NPM_TOKEN is NOT scoped here):**
```
GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/{environment_name}/secrets
```

**List environments:**
```
GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments
```

All calls require a `repo`-scoped token; `gh auth status` should already satisfy this for the maintainer account.

### Known-good JSON payloads

**Payload A — create `release` environment with required reviewer:**

```json
{
  "wait_timer": 0,
  "prevent_self_review": false,
  "reviewers": [
    { "type": "User", "id": <REPLACE_WITH_MAINTAINER_USER_ID> }
  ],
  "deployment_branch_policy": {
    "protected_branches": false,
    "custom_branch_policies": true
  }
}
```

Field notes:
- `wait_timer: 0` — no artificial delay; approval is the only gate.
- `prevent_self_review: false` — **critical for solo-maintainer.** If set to `true`, the maintainer who triggers the workflow cannot approve it, which means no one can, which means the gate deadlocks. Revisit only when a second reviewer joins.
- `reviewers` — `type` must be exactly `"User"` or `"Team"`. `id` is the numeric user/team id, not the username. Fetch with `gh api users/armelhbobdad --jq .id`.
- `deployment_branch_policy: protected_branches: false` + `custom_branch_policies: true` — these two booleans are mutually exclusive; the combination here enables the custom-list branch scope (populated by the second call below). Using `protected_branches: true` would also work since `main` is protected, but `custom_branch_policies: true` gives tighter control and matches the AC #5 language.

**Payload B — add `main` to the allowed deployment branches (second call, after Payload A):**

```json
{ "name": "main" }
```

input.new_string:
### Change Log

| Date       | Commit    | Summary                                                                                |
|------------|-----------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 2026-04-20 | `5f37053` | `feat(releasing): create release GitHub environment with required-reviewer gate`       |

### Review Findings

_Code review on 2026-04-20 against `dev` baseline (3 commits on `feat/release-workflow`). Three adversarial layers: Blind Hunter, Edge Case Hunter, Acceptance Auditor. 5 patch, 7 defer, 11 dismissed._

- [ ] [Review][Patch] `<POLICY_ID>` in DELETE snippet is a bash redirection token, not a placeholder [docs/RELEASING.md:138] — copy-pasting `gh api --method DELETE .../deployment-branch-policies/<POLICY_ID>` without substitution errors as `bash: POLICY_ID: No such file or directory`. Capture via `POLICY_ID=$(gh api ... --jq .id)` in the add step and reference as `"$POLICY_ID"` in the delete step so the block is mechanically safe; also addresses the "capture the returned policy id" step having no scripted capture, which is the mechanism that lets a feature branch persist on the allow-list if the add-output scrolls away
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Restore snippet `jq '{rules, bypass_actors}'` drops required PUT fields and contradicts the paragraph immediately below it [docs/RELEASING.md:55-65] — the ruleset `PUT` requires `name`, `target`, `enforcement`, `conditions` in addition to `rules`/`bypass_actors`; the doc itself then says "fetch current state, modify the in-memory copy, and `PUT` the complete list". Use `jq '{name, target, enforcement, conditions, rules, bypass_actors}'` or drop the `jq` filter entirely and `PUT` the full baseline
- [ ] [Review][Patch] README "Release Process" blurb omits the Release Environment / required-reviewer gate (AC #7 discoverability) [README.md:180] — current wording lists "branch-protection rules, required status checks, and (later) the rollback playbook"; Story 1.2's release-environment section is now the load-bearing piece and should be mentioned so maintainers can find it via the README
- [ ] [Review][Patch] CODEOWNERS self-review claim misstates the GitHub mechanism [docs/RELEASING.md:39] — "GitHub disallows self-review as a code owner" conflates the general PR-author-cannot-self-approve rule with a code-owner-specific restriction. The deadlock for solo-maintainer + CODEOWNERS is real, but the mechanism is "author cannot approve their own PR" (applies to everyone). Rephrase for accuracy
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Two-call restore is not idempotent — step 2 returns 422 on duplicate and the doc has no re-run guidance [docs/RELEASING.md:103-121] — `POST .../deployment-branch-policies` with `{"name": "main"}` errors if `main` is already in the allow-list. An incident responder copy-pasting the block a second time will see a confusing 422. Either note the expected 422-on-duplicate, or `DELETE` existing policies before the `POST`, or check first via `GET`

- [x] [Review][Defer] Hard-coded ruleset id `13855503` and environment id `14347249917` lack a re-derive path [docs/RELEASING.md:14,69] — if either resource is deleted and recreated the id changes and every hard-coded URL 404s; defer a follow-up that swaps the id references for name-based lookups (`gh api repos/.../rulesets --jq '.[] | select(.name=="Default") | .id'`). — deferred, cross-cuts Story 4.1 rollback playbook
- [x] [Review][Defer] "Zero env secrets" invariant has no CI assertion [docs/RELEASING.md:77,82] — doc says "re-audit"; a pre-release CI check calling `/environments/release/secrets` and failing on `total_count > 0` would turn prose into an enforced gate. — deferred, belongs with the release.yaml workflow (Story 3.1)
- [x] [Review][Defer] Required-status-checks list drifts silently if `quality.yaml` job names or matrix values change [docs/RELEASING.md:26-34] — doc warns itself ("update both in the same PR") but nothing enforces it; a pre-commit or CI step could diff the two lists. — deferred, tooling improvement outside Epic 1 scope
- [x] [Review][Defer] `prevent_self_review: false` → `true` flip on second-reviewer onboarding relies on prose memory [docs/RELEASING.md:80] — no reminder anywhere that fires when a second reviewer is added. — deferred, low probability until solo-maintainer changes
- [x] [Review][Defer] `gh auth` scopes and `jq` dependency are unstated preconditions [docs/RELEASING.md:47-65,126-140] — a maintainer with a read-only token sees opaque 403s; `jq` absence on a fresh macOS machine breaks the restore block. — deferred, prepend a "Preconditions" subsection in a later polish pass
- [x] [Review][Defer] Baseline.json capture procedure unspecified [docs/RELEASING.md:52-65] — restore is aspirational if no baseline has been captured; needs a companion capture command and a guidance on where baselines live. — deferred, Story 4.1 rollback playbook is the natural home
- [x] [Review][Defer] Site-nav discoverability of `docs/RELEASING.md` (Starlight/Astro sidebar) unverified [docs/RELEASING.md:1-4] — frontmatter is Starlight-compatible but no sidebar entry was audited; if the docs site is built with Starlight, readers arriving at the site may not find the page without a direct URL. — deferred, confirm docs-site build config before acting

content.text:
## Code review complete

**0 decision-needed, 5 patch, 7 defer, 11 dismissed as noise.**

Findings written to the `### Review Findings` section of `_bmad-output/implementation-artifacts/1-2-create-release-github-environment-with-required-reviewer.md`. Defers also appended to `_bmad-output/implementation-artifacts/deferred-work.md`.

### Patch findings (5)

1. **`<POLICY_ID>` is a bash redirection token, not a placeholder** — `docs/RELEASING.md:138`. Copy-paste of the revoke command errors before it runs. Capture via `POLICY_ID=$(gh api ... --jq .id)` and reference as `"$POLICY_ID"`.
2. **Restore snippet `jq '{rules, bypass_actors}'` drops required PUT fields and contradicts its own paragraph** — `docs/RELEASING.md:55-65`. PUT requires `name`, `target`, `enforcement`, `conditions` too.
3. **README "Release Process" blurb omits the Release Environment gate** — `README.md:180`. AC #7 discoverability hole.
4. **CODEOWNERS self-review claim misstates the mechanism** — `docs/RELEASING.md:39`. The rule is "author cannot self-approve" (applies to everyone), not code-owner-specific.
5. **Two-call restore returns 422 on duplicate, no re-run guidance** — `docs/RELEASING.md:103-121`. Step 2 `POST` of an existing `main` entry errors confusingly during incident response.

### How would you like to handle the 5 `patch` findings?

1. **Fix them automatically** — I will apply fixes now
2. **Leave as action items** — they are already in the story file
3. **Walk through each** — let me show details before deciding

HALT — reply with only the number.

content.content:
50	### Restore from a saved baseline
51	
52	A JSON baseline captured before any change can be replayed via:
53	
54	```bash
55	# Extract rules + bypass_actors from a saved baseline.json
56	jq '{rules, bypass_actors}' baseline.json > /tmp/restore.json
57	
58	gh api --method PUT \
59	  repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets/13855503 \
60	  --input /tmp/restore.json
61	```
62	
63	The GitHub ruleset API uses `PUT` (not `PATCH`) for updates, and the `rules` array is replaced wholesale — it is not merged server-side. Always fetch current state, modify the in-memory copy, and `PUT` the complete list.
64	
65	## Release Environment
66	
67	The publish job is gated by a **GitHub [deployment environment](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/deployment/targeting-different-environments/using-environments-for-deployment) with a required-reviewer rule** (not by workflow logic). A job declaring `environment: release` pauses until a listed reviewer clicks "Approve and deploy" in the Actions UI.
68	
69	**Environment:** `release` — id `14347249917` — created 2026-04-20.
70	
71	| Setting                      | Value                                               |
72	|------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|
73	| `wait_timer`                 | `0` (no artificial delay; approval is the only gate) |
74	| `prevent_self_review`        | `false` — see rationale below                       |
75	| `reviewers`                  | `armelhbobdad` (user id `132626034`), 1 approver    |
76	| `deployment_branch_policy`   | `custom_branch_policies: true`, list: `main` only   |
77	| Environment-scoped secrets   | `0` (invariant — see `NPM_TOKEN` note below)        |
78	| Cost                         | `$0` on public-repo tier (environments, required reviewers, and branch policies are [free for public repositories](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/deployment/targeting-different-environments/using-environments-for-deployment#about-environments)) |
79	
80	**`prevent_self_review: false` — correctness constraint, not a loosened control.** Solo-maintainer setups cannot self-approve when this is `true`, so the gate would deadlock on any maintainer-triggered publish. The value flips to `true` the moment a second reviewer joins — do not leave it loose by inertia.
81	
82	**`NPM_TOKEN` is NOT scoped to this environment.** It remains at repo-level secrets during the OIDC transition and is removed entirely post-v1.0.0 (see Story 6.3). The invariant: the `release` env must have zero secrets. If a future change scopes any secret here, re-audit whether the OIDC trusted-publisher path is still in force.
83	
84	**Coupling with npm trusted publishing (Story 1.3).** The npm trusted publisher binds on four fields — `organization=armelhbobdad`, `repository=bmad-module-skill-forge`, `workflow filename=release.yaml`, `environment=release`. The environment name above is load-bearing: any rename here must be accompanied by a matching npm-side update in the same change, or the next publish returns 404.
85	
86	### Inspect current state
87	
88	```bash
89	# Environment itself (reviewers, branch-policy shape, prevent_self_review)
90	gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release
91	
92	# Allowed deployment branches (expect: one entry, name "main")
93	gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies
94	
95	# Environment-scoped secrets (expect: zero)
96	gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/secrets
97	```
98	
99	### Restore / re-apply
100	
101	Two-call pattern — the environment and its branch-policy list are separate resources. Creating the environment alone with `custom_branch_policies: true` leaves the allow-list empty, which rejects every dispatch; the second call is mandatory.
102	
103	```bash
104	# (1) Create or update the environment with the required-reviewer gate
105	gh api --method PUT repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release --input - <<'JSON'
106	{
107	  "wait_timer": 0,
108	  "prevent_self_review": false,
109	  "reviewers": [{ "type": "User", "id": 132626034 }],
110	  "deployment_branch_policy": {
111	    "protected_branches": false,
112	    "custom_branch_policies": true
113	  }
114	}
115	JSON
116	
117	# (2) Add main to the allowed deployment branches
118	gh api --method POST repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies --input - <<'JSON'
119	{ "name": "main" }
120	JSON
121	```
122	
123	### Temporarily allowing a feature branch
124	
125	A release workflow that declares `environment: release` will be rejected from any branch not on the allow-list. For legitimate validation cuts from a feature branch (Story 3.2's alpha cut is the canonical case), widen the allow-list for the duration of the test and tighten it back immediately:
126	
127	```bash
128	# Allow the feature branch (capture the returned policy id)
129	gh api --method POST \
130	  repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies \
131	  --input - <<'JSON'
132	{ "name": "feat/my-validation-branch" }
133	JSON
134	# → { "id": <POLICY_ID>, "name": "feat/my-validation-branch", ... }
135	
136	# ... run the validation cut, approve via the reviewer UI, confirm publish ...
137	
138	# Revoke immediately — the allow-list must return to { main } before leaving the session
139	gh api --method DELETE \
140	  repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies/<POLICY_ID>
141	```
142	
143	Leaving a feature branch on the allow-list is an unguarded hole — any later `workflow_dispatch` from that branch would be able to reach the publish path. Treat the revoke as the last step of the validation, not a follow-up.
144	
145	<!-- Rollback Playbook — added in Story 4.1 -->
146	
147	## Rollback Playbook
148	
149	_To be authored in Story 4.1 — rollback playbook covering scenarios such as failed publish, tag mismatch, bad `latest` dist-tag, OIDC auth failure, and post-publish security issue._

### Restore from a saved baseline

A JSON baseline captured before any change can be replayed via:

```bash
# Extract rules + bypass_actors from a saved baseline.json
jq '{rules, bypass_actors}' baseline.json > /tmp/restore.json

gh api --method PUT \
  repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/rulesets/13855503 \
  --input /tmp/restore.json
```

The GitHub ruleset API uses `PUT` (not `PATCH`) for updates, and the `rules` array is replaced wholesale — it is not merged server-side. Always fetch current state, modify the in-memory copy, and `PUT` the complete list.

## Release Environment

The publish job is gated by a **GitHub [deployment environment](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/deployment/targeting-different-environments/using-environments-for-deployment) with a required-reviewer rule** (not by workflow logic). A job declaring `environment: release` pauses until a listed reviewer clicks "Approve and deploy" in the Actions UI.

**Environment:** `release` — id `14347249917` — created 2026-04-20.

| Setting                      | Value                                               |
|------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| `wait_timer`                 | `0` (no artificial delay; approval is the only gate) |
| `prevent_self_review`        | `false` — see rationale below                       |
| `reviewers`                  | `armelhbobdad` (user id `132626034`), 1 approver    |
| `deployment_branch_policy`   | `custom_branch_policies: true`, list: `main` only   |
| Environment-scoped secrets   | `0` (invariant — see `NPM_TOKEN` note below)        |
| Cost                         | `$0` on public-repo tier (environments, required reviewers, and branch policies are [free for public repositories](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/deployment/targeting-different-environments/using-environments-for-deployment#about-environments)) |

**`prevent_self_review: false` — correctness constraint, not a loosened control.** Solo-maintainer setups cannot self-approve when this is `true`, so the gate would deadlock on any maintainer-triggered publish. The value flips to `true` the moment a second reviewer joins — do not leave it loose by inertia.

**`NPM_TOKEN` is NOT scoped to this environment.** It remains at repo-level secrets during the OIDC transition and is removed entirely post-v1.0.0 (see Story 6.3). The invariant: the `release` env must have zero secrets. If a future change scopes any secret here, re-audit whether the OIDC trusted-publisher path is still in force.

**Coupling with npm trusted publishing (Story 1.3).** The npm trusted publisher binds on four fields — `organization=armelhbobdad`, `repository=bmad-module-skill-forge`, `workflow filename=release.yaml`, `environment=release`. The environment name above is load-bearing: any rename here must be accompanied by a matching npm-side update in the same change, or the next publish returns 404.

### Inspect current state

```bash
# Environment itself (reviewers, branch-policy shape, prevent_self_review)
gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release

# Allowed deployment branches (expect: one entry, name "main")
gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies

# Environment-scoped secrets (expect: zero)
gh api repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/secrets
```

### Restore / re-apply

Two-call pattern — the environment and its branch-policy list are separate resources. Creating the environment alone with `custom_branch_policies: true` leaves the allow-list empty, which rejects every dispatch; the second call is mandatory.

```bash
# (1) Create or update the environment with the required-reviewer gate
gh api --method PUT repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release --input - <<'JSON'
{
  "wait_timer": 0,
  "prevent_self_review": false,
  "reviewers": [{ "type": "User", "id": 132626034 }],
  "deployment_branch_policy": {
    "protected_branches": false,
    "custom_branch_policies": true
  }
}
JSON

# (2) Add main to the allowed deployment branches
gh api --method POST repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies --input - <<'JSON'
{ "name": "main" }
JSON
```

### Temporarily allowing a feature branch

A release workflow that declares `environment: release` will be rejected from any branch not on the allow-list. For legitimate validation cuts from a feature branch (Story 3.2's alpha cut is the canonical case), widen the allow-list for the duration of the test and tighten it back immediately:

```bash
# Allow the feature branch (capture the returned policy id)
gh api --method POST \
  repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies \
  --input - <<'JSON'
{ "name": "feat/my-validation-branch" }
JSON
# → { "id": <POLICY_ID>, "name": "feat/my-validation-branch", ... }

# ... run the validation cut, approve via the reviewer UI, confirm publish ...

# Revoke immediately — the allow-list must return to { main } before leaving the session
gh api --method DELETE \
  repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies/<POLICY_ID>
```

Leaving a feature branch on the allow-list is an unguarded hole — any later `workflow_dispatch` from that branch would be able to reach the publish path. Treat the revoke as the last step of the validation, not a follow-up.

<!-- Rollback Playbook — added in Story 4.1 -->

## Rollback Playbook

_To be authored in Story 4.1 — rollback playbook covering scenarios such as failed publish, tag mismatch, bad `latest` dist-tag, OIDC auth failure, and post-publish security issue._

35	
36	**Coupling with `quality.yaml`:** if that workflow renames a job or changes the `strategy.matrix.os` for `validate` or `python`, the ruleset's `required_status_checks` list must be updated in lock-step — otherwise merges to `main` will either block on a check name that no longer reports, or silently pass without the renamed check. Update both in the same PR.
37	
38	**`strict_required_status_checks_policy: false`** — PR branches are not forced to be up-to-date with `main` before merging. This avoids constant rebases on a low-traffic repo. Flip to `true` if concurrent merges start producing logical conflicts the checks can't catch.
39	
40	**CODEOWNERS note:** the `pull_request` rule has `require_code_owner_review: true`, but no `.github/CODEOWNERS` file exists in the repo today. GitHub treats the code-owner requirement as vacuously satisfied when the file is absent, so this setting is currently a no-op — the only active review gate is `required_approving_review_count: 1`. If a CODEOWNERS file is added later, make sure the listed owners can actually approve PRs from other authors (GitHub disallows self-review as a code owner, which would deadlock solo-maintainer PRs).
41	
42	**Bypass actors:** `RepositoryRole` actor_id=5 (Admin), `bypass_mode: pull_request`. Admins can bypass the ruleset **only via a pull request**, never via direct push. This preserves `non_fast_forward` and the required-checks gate for the `github-actions[bot]` account that the future `release.yaml` workflow (Story 3.1) will use to push tags and commits to `main`. **Do not add a bot-specific bypass**; it would defeat the whole purpose of this ruleset.
43

48	```

**Coupling with `quality.yaml`:** if that workflow renames a job or changes the `strategy.matrix.os` for `validate` or `python`, the ruleset's `required_status_checks` list must be updated in lock-step — otherwise merges to `main` will either block on a check name that no longer reports, or silently pass without the renamed check. Update both in the same PR.

**`strict_required_status_checks_policy: false`** — PR branches are not forced to be up-to-date with `main` before merging. This avoids constant rebases on a low-traffic repo. Flip to `true` if concurrent merges start producing logical conflicts the checks can't catch.
